questions.question: What is database normalization and why is it important?
questions.explanation: Normalization organizes data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity. Normal forms: 1NF (atomic values), 2NF (no partial dependencies), 3NF (no transitive dependencies). Prevents anomalies (insertion, update, deletion). Tradeoff: normalized databases may require more joins.

questions.question: Explain ACID properties.
questions.explanation: Atomicity: transaction all-or-nothing. Consistency: data valid before/after. Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere. Durability: committed data persists. Critical for data integrity.

questions.question: What is indexing and what are its tradeoffs?
questions.explanation: Indexes speed up query retrieval by creating pointers to data. Types: B-tree, hash, bitmap. Tradeoffs: faster reads but slower writes (index updates), additional storage required. Choose indexes based on query patterns.

questions.question: Explain the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases.
questions.explanation: SQL: relational, ACID compliant, structured schema, joins. NoSQL: flexible schema, horizontal scaling, eventual consistency. SQL for structured data with complex relationships, NoSQL for unstructured/semi-structured data at scale.

questions.question: What is database sharding?
questions.explanation: Sharding partitions data across multiple databases/servers based on shard key. Enables horizontal scaling and parallel processing. Challenges: maintaining referential integrity, handling hotspots, rebalancing. Most NoSQL databases have built-in sharding support.
